systems.note: Raw all-MiniLM-L6-v2 cosine + numpy argsort. The lower bound for dense retrieval — no scoring, no tier awareness.
STMSemanticRetriever with the composite scorer ablated (weights all on relevance). Isolates how much lift the scorer adds on top of pure-cosine ordering.
STMSemanticRetriever with default composite scorer (relevance 0.45 / importance 0.25 / recency 0.20 / confidence 0.10). The eval-used Continuum retriever.

skipped: faiss-cpu not installed. On this corpus size (<1K vectors), FAISS HNSW would be near-identical to brute-force cosine — its value is at the 10K+ scale where naive numpy stops fitting in cache.
skipped: requires Postgres infrastructure. Continuum's PostgresMTM/LTM already use pgvector — see tests/integration/test_pgvector_upgrade.py for the integration smoke that exercises it under load.
skipped: creates billed remote resources (vector store + assistant) and would falsify a free local benchmark. A separate follow-up can add it behind an explicit --enable-paid flag.

narrative: On this synthetic corpus of 200 sessions × 30 queries with vocabulary-overlapping distractors, recall@4 lands at: naive_cosine 55.0%, continuum_topk_only 55.0%, continuum_stm 55.0%. Composite scorer over pure cosine: +0.0pp; Continuum over raw cosine: +0.0pp. The scorer ties because this corpus has near-uniform timestamps and no importance variation — its recency / importance signals have nothing to differentiate on. On a production corpus with real temporal spread the lift would surface; here the benchmark isolates pure-relevance retrieval. Median latency: naive 5.4ms, continuum 7.1ms (+32% overhead for tier-aware retrieval + metadata indexing); retrieval is dominated by the embedder forward pass.
